chunk_summary: Vitamin D and calcium replacement are essential for treating nutritional rickets, which can coexist with stunting and cause orthopedic complications. Screening, treatment, and follow-up are critical for effective management.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of vitamin D and calcium in managing nutritional rickets and supporting linear growth.
Learn the clinical and biochemical screening methods for stunted children with bone symptoms.
Familiarize with pediatric dosing regimens for vitamin D and calcium supplementation.
Recognize non-nutritional causes of rickets and appropriate referral protocols.
key_facts: Vitamin D deficiency and low calcium intake can contribute to stunting and bone symptoms like wrist widening and waddling gait.
Pediatric treatment includes daily cholecalciferol: 2000 IU/day for infants under 12 months and 3000 IU/day for older children, alongside age-appropriate calcium supplementation.
Supervised 'stoss' dosing may be used in settings with adherence challenges, but requires monitoring for toxicity.
Reassess clinically and biochemically after 4–6 weeks to confirm symptom improvement and falling alkaline phosphatase levels.
Maintenance therapy includes sustained dietary calcium through milk/curd and continued vitamin D supplementation.
reference_quote: Global consensus recommendations suggest daily cholecalciferol dosing of 2000 IU for infants and 3000 IU for older children, alongside calcium supplementation for nutritional rickets management. (Source: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26593520/)
document_key_topics: Vitamin D and calcium replacement for nutritional rickets
Screening and biochemical markers for stunted children
Pediatric dosing regimens for rickets treatment
